beginner.question: What does the z-index property do in CSS?
beginner.answer: z-index controls the stacking order of positioned elements along the z-axis (depth — perpendicular to the screen). Elements with higher z-index values appear in front of (on top of) elements with lower values. For example, z-index: 10 appears above z-index: 5. z-index only works on positioned elements (position: relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky) and flex/grid items.

beginner.question: What is the default z-index value?
beginner.answer: The default z-index value is auto. Elements with z-index: auto inherit the stacking context of their parent and are stacked according to their position in the DOM order (later elements appear on top of earlier ones). Elements with z-index: auto do NOT create a new stacking context.

beginner.question: Can z-index have negative values and what do they mean?
beginner.answer: Yes, z-index can be negative (e.g., z-index: -1). A negative value places the element behind normal-flow elements and behind the background of its parent stacking context. For example, z-index: -1 puts an element behind its parent's background but in front of the parent element's background if the parent creates a stacking context.

beginner.question: Why does z-index sometimes not work even when you set a value?
beginner.answer: The most common reason is that z-index only works on positioned elements (elements with position set to relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky, not static). If you set z-index on a statically-positioned element, it has no effect. Additionally, if a parent creates a stacking context, a child's z-index only matters within that parent's context, not globally.

beginner.question: What is a stacking context?
beginner.answer: A stacking context is a group of elements that share a common parent and are painted together in a specific order from back to front. Elements within one stacking context cannot overlap elements in another stacking context — the entire context is treated as a single unit. Stacking contexts isolate their children from the rest of the page.

beginner.question: How does the DOM order affect z-index when no z-index is set?
beginner.answer: When elements don't have explicit z-index values (or have z-index: auto), the DOM order determines stacking: elements that appear later in the HTML are painted later and appear on top of earlier elements. This is the natural stacking order without z-index interference.

beginner.question: What is the difference between z-index: auto and z-index: 0?
beginner.answer: Both auto and 0 place the element at the same stack level (the parent's stack level). However, z-index: 0 creates a new stacking context, while z-index: auto does NOT. This means child elements with z-index: 0 form a stacking boundary, while children of z-index: auto can escape outside the parent's stacking context.

beginner.question: What are common use cases for high z-index values?
beginner.answer: Common use cases include: modals/dialogs (z-index: 500-1000), overlays/backdrops (z-index: 400-900), fixed headers/navbars (z-index: 100-300), dropdown menus (z-index: 50-100), tooltips (z-index: 100-200), and toast notifications (z-index: 500-1000). A suggested scale: dropdown 100, sticky 200, fixed 300, overlay 400, modal 500, toast 600, tooltip 700.

beginner.question: Does z-index work on flex items?
beginner.answer: Yes, z-index works on flex items without needing position: relative. Flex items (direct children of a flex container) can use z-index directly, and they also create stacking contexts when z-index is set. This is an exception to the rule that z-index only works on positioned elements — flex items can use z-index with their default position: static.

beginner.question: How do you make one element appear on top of another?
beginner.answer: Ensure both elements are positioned (position: relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky), then give the element you want on top a higher z-index value: .element1 { position: relative; z-index: 1; } .element2 { position: relative; z-index: 2; } /* element2 is on top */. If they share the same stacking context, the higher z-index wins.

intermediate.question: What properties create a new stacking context besides position + z-index?
intermediate.answer: Properties that create stacking contexts include: (1) opacity less than 1; (2) transform other than none; (3) filter other than none; (4) mix-blend-mode other than normal; (5) isolation: isolate; (6) -webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch; (7) will-change with any value that creates a stacking context; (8) contain: layout/paint/style; (9) perspective other than none; (10) clip-path other than none; (11) mask/mask-image other than none.

intermediate.question: How does opacity less than 1 create a stacking context and how can it cause z-index issues?
intermediate.answer: When an element has opacity less than 1, it creates a new stacking context — any positioned children with their own z-index values are confined to that opacity-created context. This means a child with z-index: 9999 inside an opacity: 0.9 parent cannot appear above elements outside that parent. This is a very common source of unexpected z-index behavior — developers set opacity for a fade-in effect and suddenly z-index stops working.

intermediate.question: What is the isolation: isolate property and how does it help with stacking contexts?
intermediate.answer: isolation: isolate explicitly creates a new stacking context without any side effects. It's the cleanest way to contain z-index within a component: .widget { isolation: isolate; }. Any z-index values inside the widget only matter within the widget — they can't leak out and affect elements outside. This is useful for component-based architectures where each component should manage its own stacking independently.

intermediate.question: How do you debug z-index issues where elements don't stack as expected?
intermediate.answer: Debugging strategies: (1) Check if z-index is applied to a positioned element (non-static position required except for flex items); (2) Inspect the element's ancestors for stacking context-creating properties (opacity, transform, filter, isolation, will-change); (3) Use DevTools 'Layers' panel (Chrome) to view all stacked layers; (4) Check if the issue is with another stacking context — a high z-index inside a low z-index context is still constrained; (5) Temporarily remove properties like opacity, transform, and filter to identify the culprit.

intermediate.question: What is the z-index scale best practice for large projects?
intermediate.answer: Use CSS custom properties to define a z-index scale: :root { --z-dropdown: 100; --z-sticky: 200; --z-fixed: 300; --z-overlay: 400; --z-modal: 500; --z-toast: 600; --z-tooltip: 700; }. This creates a predictable, organized system. Avoid inline z-index values (especially large ones like 99999). Group values by purpose (UI overlays, navigation, content) rather than context-specific naming. A scale with evenly spaced increments (e.g., 100, 200, 300) allows inserting new layers without renumbering everything.

intermediate.question: How does z-index interact with 3D transforms (transform: translateZ)?
intermediate.answer: When using 3D transforms (transform: translateZ()), z-index interacts with the 3D rendering context. Elements with higher translateZ values appear closer to the viewer, similar to z-index but in 3D space. The perspective property creates the 3D space. Elements can have both z-index and translateZ — they combine to determine the final visual stacking. 3D transforms create their own stacking context, so z-index inside a 3D transformed element only applies within that context.

intermediate.question: How does z-index work with position: sticky elements?
intermediate.answer: position: sticky elements are treated as positioned elements for z-index purposes. They can have z-index set directly without needing an additional position value. However, sticky elements within different stacking contexts stack according to their context, not just their z-index. Sticky elements often need explicit z-index to ensure they appear above content when 'stuck.' Additionally, sticky elements within scrolled containers may be clipped by the container's stacking context.

intermediate.question: What is the stacking order within a single stacking context (paint order)?
intermediate.answer: Within a stacking context, elements are painted from back to front in this order: (1) Background and borders of the context-forming element; (2) Elements with negative z-index (and their children); (3) Non-positioned, non-floated block-level elements in DOM order; (4) Non-positioned floated elements; (5) Inline elements, inline-block, and inline-table elements; (6) Positioned elements with z-index: auto/0, and child stacking contexts with z-index: 0/auto, in DOM order; (7) Elements with positive z-index (lowest first).

intermediate.question: How does z-index interact with full-screen mode (element.requestFullscreen())?
intermediate.answer: When an element enters full-screen mode, the browser renders it on top of everything else, effectively giving it the highest possible z-index. The full-screen pseudo-class (:fullscreen) can be used to style the full-screen element. The element receives a stacking context at the top of the viewport — no z-index value can place an element above it. This is important for video players and presentation tools.

intermediate.question: What is the relationship between z-index and the CSS contain property?
intermediate.answer: contain: layout creates a new stacking context for its contents, isolating them from the rest of the page. contain: paint also creates a stacking context and clips positioned descendants. contain: style creates a stacking context for counters and quotes. This means using containment for performance automatically affects z-index behavior — children can't break out of the contained context. This is generally fine for isolated components but can break global overlays (modals, tooltips) if they're inside a contained element.

advanced.question: Explain the complete algorithm for painting order in a stacking context, including CSS Shadow DOM and iframes.
advanced.answer: The painting algorithm for a stacking context, from back to front: (1) background and borders of the context element; (2) descendant stacking contexts with negative stack levels (most negative first); (3) in-flow, non-inline-level, non-positioned descendants (block boxes) in DOM order; (4) non-positioned floats; (5) in-flow, inline-level, non-positioned descendants (inline boxes) in DOM order; (6) positioned descendants and child stacking contexts with z-index: auto/0; (7) descendant stacking contexts with positive stack levels (lowest first). Shadow DOM v1 slot elements paint within their host's stacking context at the appropriate level. iframes have their own stacking contexts and appear at the z-index level of the iframe element itself.

advanced.question: How do you create a z-index management system for a micro-frontend or component-based architecture?
advanced.answer: Use CSS custom properties and isolation: each component root has isolation: isolate to create a clean stacking context boundary. The application manages a global z-index scale via custom properties. Components declare their required z-index relative to their local context: .modal { z-index: var(--z-modal, 100); }. The parent application sets the scale: :root { --z-dropdown: 100; --z-overlay: 500; --z-modal: 1000; }. This prevents components from accidentally breaking each other's stacking while maintaining a consistent z-index language across independently developed components.

advanced.question: How does z-index interact with the Popover API and top-layer elements (dialog, popover)?
advanced.answer: The Popover API and HTML dialog's showModal() place elements in the 'top layer' — a special stacking layer that sits ABOVE all other content regardless of z-index. Top-layer elements (like <dialog> opened with showModal(), or elements with popover attribute) are rendered in a separate layer that's not part of the document's stacking context tree. This means no z-index value can place an element above a top-layer element, and vice versa. CSS cannot style the order between top-layer elements — their stacking is determined by the order they were opened.

advanced.question: How does the CSS specification handle z-index when an element is inside multiple nested stacking contexts?
advanced.answer: An element's effective stack level is relative to its parent stacking context, not the root. A child with z-index: 999 inside a parent with z-index: 1 only appears above siblings within that parent context, but the entire parent context (z-index: 1) is below a sibling context with z-index: 2. This is the most common z-index bug — thinking a high z-index on a child makes it globally on top, ignoring that parent stacking contexts limit the child's scope.

advanced.question: How does the browser's rendering engine composite layers differently for elements with high z-index vs animations?
advanced.answer: The browser creates composited layers for elements that need to be painted independently, including those with high z-index (especially when combined with position: fixed or transform). Elements with will-change: transform, CSS animations, and certain z-index values get promoted to their own GPU compositor layer. Multiple high-z-index elements can create many composited layers, increasing memory usage. The browser must composite these layers in the correct order, and too many layers can hit the GPU texture memory limit on mobile devices.

advanced.question: How does z-index interact with CSS scroll-driven animations and view transitions?
advanced.answer: Scroll-driven animations (@scroll-timeline) and CSS View Transitions create their own stacking contexts during the transition/animation. During a view transition, the old and new page states exist in separate stacking contexts above normal content. Elements with z-index during scroll-driven animations maintain their normal z-index relationships but within the animation's context. The animation's stacking context is determined by the animated element's z-index, not the animation itself. View transitions specifically create the '::view-transition-old' and '::view-transition-new' pseudo-elements in a dedicated top-layer-like context.

advanced.question: How do you debug stacking context issues in complex applications with dozens of overlapping components?
advanced.answer: Advanced debugging: (1) Chrome DevTools 'Layers' tab shows all composited layers with their reasons (z-index, transform, etc.); (2) Use 'Rendering' → 'Layer borders' to visualize composited layers as colored borders; (3) Paint flashing shows areas being repainted; (4) Console: getComputedStyle(element).z-index to verify computed value; (5) Temporarily apply outline: 1px solid red to all elements to visualize boundaries without layout shift; (6) Add isolation: isolate step by step to contain suspicious stacking; (7) Use the Element panel's 'Stacking context' section (Chrome) to see an element's stacking context tree.

advanced.question: How does z-index work with the CSS cascade layers (@layer) and does @layer affect stacking context creation?
advanced.answer: CSS cascade layers (@layer) control the cascade priority of declarations but do NOT affect stacking contexts or z-index behavior. The z-index property still follows the same rules regardless of which @layer the declaration is in. However, @layer can help organize z-index values by placing them in a specific layer order. For example, you could have a 'base' layer with low z-index utilities and an 'overlays' layer with high z-index components — but the actual z-index values still determine stacking, not the @layer order.

advanced.question: Explain the relationship between z-index and the CSS backdrop-filter property.
advanced.answer: backdrop-filter applies a graphical effect (blur, color shift, etc.) to the area BEHIND an element. Elements with backdrop-filter create a new stacking context (similar to opacity < 1 or transform). This means: (1) The element and its children are isolated in their own context; (2) The backdrop-filter effect only applies to content beneath this stacking context; (3) z-index inside a backdrop-filter element is relative to this new context. This is why frosted-glass effects (backdrop-filter: blur) require careful z-index management — the glass element must be positioned above the content it should blur.

advanced.question: How does z-index interact with the Inert attribute and the browser's focus order?
advanced.answer: The inert attribute makes elements uninteractive (no focus, no clicks). While inert doesn't directly affect z-index, elements with inert are typically hidden or made non-interactive. Combined with z-index issues: an element with high z-index but inert is visible but non-interactive. This can create confusing user experiences where a modal appears on top (high z-index) but is inert (can't be closed) because inert was applied incorrectly. Additionally, the browser's focus order (Tab key) follows DOM order, not z-index order — an element with high z-index that's later in the DOM may receive focus after elements visually behind it.
